record_office_terminology.land_records: Recorder of Deeds (county-level deed and mortgage records)
record_office_terminology.probate: Register of Wills / Orphans Court (county-level probate jurisdiction)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Orphans Court (marriage licenses from 1885 statewide)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1906 statewide. Philadelphia from 1860. Marriage licenses from 1885.

burned_county_authority: Pennsylvania has outstanding record survival. Franklin County (Chambersburg burned by Confederates 1864) is the only county with significant fire-related losses, though courthouse records survived. No other PA counties are classified as burned counties. The PA State Archives in Harrisburg serves as a backup repository for many county records through microfilm and digitization programs.
administrative_notes: Pennsylvania was founded by William Penn in 1682 as a Quaker colony. One of the 13 original colonies and 2nd state to ratify the Constitution (1787). The original three counties were Bucks, Chester, and Philadelphia (1682). Pennsylvania has 67 counties. Philadelphia is both a city and county (consolidated 1854). County offices include Recorder of Deeds (land), Register of Wills (probate), Prothonotary (court), and Register and Recorder (combined in some counties). The PA State Archives in Harrisburg is the central repository for state-level records. Penn's unique warrant-survey-patent land system differs from other states. Statewide vital registration began in 1906; marriage licenses from 1885. PA had gradual abolition of slavery from 1780 (first abolition act in US).

core_repositories.repository: PA Historical and Museum Commission, Harrisburg
core_repositories.description: Central repository for PA state records. Holdings include colonial records, land records (warrant-survey-patent), vital records (deaths 1906-1968, births 1906-1913, marriages 1885-1963), military records, and county microfilm collections. Essential starting point for all PA research.
Major genealogical collection including published genealogies, county histories, city directories, newspapers on microfilm, and the Power Library digitized collections.

One of America's premier manuscript repositories. Holdings include colonial records, Quaker records, family papers, Balch Institute immigration archives, genealogical card indexes, and thousands of manuscript collections spanning 1680s to present.
Premier PA genealogical society providing indexed databases, research guides, and publications. Online databases include vital records indexes, church records, and family files.

World-class Quaker archive holding Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records from 1680s. Birth, marriage, death, disownment, and removal records for Bucks, Chester, Delaware, Montgomery, Philadelphia counties and beyond.
Premier genealogical society for western PA. Extensive surname files, church record abstracts, cemetery indexes, and publications covering Allegheny and surrounding counties.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive PA collections including church records, tax lists, probate records, land records, vital records, and census records. Massive microfilm collections from PA counties.
Comprehensive research guide covering PA resources by county, record type, and time period.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Free digitized historic PA newspapers. Hundreds of titles covering every region of the state. Essential for obituaries, vital records, and legal notices.
Subscription access to thousands of digitized PA newspapers. Philadelphia Inquirer, Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, and hundreds of county papers.

military_records.description: Civil War muster rolls (digitized), Revolutionary War records, War of 1812 records, militia rolls, and National Guard records. PA Archives published series contains extensive military documentation.
Digitized federal military records including pension files, service records, and compiled records for PA soldiers from all wars.

land_and_property.description: Penn's proprietary land system: warrants (application to purchase), surveys (measurement), patents (final grant). Unique to PA. Records from 1681. Digitization in progress.
Each of PA's 67 county recorders maintains deed and mortgage records. Many now have online indexes (LANDEX in several counties). Records date from colonial era in early counties.

vital_records.source_name: PA State Archives — Free Vital Records
vital_records.description: Free online access to death certificates (1906-1968), birth certificates (1906-1913), and marriage license applications (1885-1963 by county). One of the best free vital records programs in the US.

vital_records.source_name: PA Department of Health Vital Records
vital_records.repository: PA Dept of Health, New Castle
vital_records.description: Current vital records. Birth certificates from 1906, death certificates from 1906, marriage and divorce records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Moravian Archives, 41 W Locust St, Bethlehem
church_and_cemetery.description: World-class archive of Moravian Church records from 1741. Daily diaries, membership rolls, correspondence documenting every community member. Essential for Lehigh Valley and beyond.
Cemetery records for PA. Extensive volunteer-contributed records with photographs covering thousands of PA cemeteries.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Research guides covering PA record types, county jurisdictions, and special collections. Essential orientation for PA research.
County-by-county research guides with record availability, repository information, and historical context.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Pennsylvania has had a stable county system since 1878 (Lackawanna, the last county created). No counties have been abolished. The original three counties (Bucks, Chester, Philadelphia) were created in 1682. All 67 current counties trace their formation from these original three through various parent county subdivisions.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: PA county boundaries have been stable since 1878. No extinct counties.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Pennsylvania passed the first abolition act in the US in 1780 (Gradual Abolition Act). Slaveholders required to register enslaved persons. Manumission records at county level. PA Abolition Society records at HSP. Free Black community records from 1780s forward.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: HSP / National Underground Railroad Freedom Center / County Historical Societies
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: PA was a major Underground Railroad corridor. Philadelphia, Chester County, and Lancaster County have extensive documentation. Mother Bethel AME Church records (1794). William Still's records of escaped enslaved persons.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Philadelphia was THE colonial immigration port. German immigrant lists from 1727 published by Strassburger and Hinke. Irish, English, and other arrivals. Second-busiest US port after New York through the 1800s.
Federal court naturalization records for Eastern and Middle Districts of Pennsylvania. County court naturalizations at individual county offices.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Balch Institute for Ethnic Studies (now at HSP)
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Massive collection documenting immigrant communities: German, Irish, Italian, Polish, Jewish, Ukrainian, Lithuanian, and dozens of other groups in PA.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Pennsylvania has exceptional record survival. Franklin County (Chambersburg burned by Confederate cavalry July 30, 1864) is the only county with significant fire-related losses, though the courthouse and most county records survived. No other PA counties have experienced major courthouse fires or record losses. The PA State Archives microfilm program has created backup copies of records from most counties.
